description: Eskom's total debt exceeds R420bn with significant concentration in government-guaranteed bonds. Without debt relief, debt service costs will exceed operating cash flows, threatening solvency.
Chronic fleet underperformance with Energy Availability Factor (EAF) below 60% causing Stage 6 load shedding. Multiple units on unplanned outage exceeding 18,000MW simultaneously at peak.

Coal supply security risk: Primary suppliers unable to meet contractual volumes. Stockpiles at 13 of 15 coal stations below the 20-day emergency threshold. Spot market procurement at premium pricing required.
Air quality non-compliance at 11 of 15 coal power stations. Minimum Emission Standards (MES) violations under the National Environmental Management: Air Quality Act. Potential AEL revocations.
Transmission unbundling execution risk: Establishing NTCSA as a separate entity involves complex asset separation, staff transfers and systems migration. Delay risks regulatory non-compliance.
Ageing ICT infrastructure and SCADA systems at power stations and control centres. Average system age exceeds 15 years. Cyber attack risk elevated following global utility sector incidents.
Critical skills shortage in power station engineering and artisan trades. Over 1,200 artisan vacancies across generation fleet. Retirement wave of experienced engineers in 2025-2027.
Port of Durban container terminal operating below 50% design capacity. Average vessel waiting time 6.8 days vs global benchmark of 24 hours. R42bn annual economic impact on SA exports estimated.

Cable theft and rail vandalism costing Transnet R5bn annually in direct losses plus R18bn in lost revenue. Northern Cape iron ore corridor and manganese line severely impacted. SAPS capacity insufficient.
Transnet total debt of R132bn with R28bn maturing within 12 months. Declining freight volumes and port inefficiencies pressuring debt service coverage ratios. Refinancing risk elevated.
New locomotive programme delivery delays (CRRC contract): 440 of 1,064 units still undelivered. Local content disputes. Rolling stock maintenance backlog on aging Transnet-owned fleet.
Third party rail access disruption risk: Opening freight rail to private operators may reduce Transnet revenue by 20-30% if not managed with appropriate tariff recovery and access charges.

Eskom load shedding impact: Each stage of load shedding reduces Transnet operational capacity by approximately 8%. Port cranes, rail signalling and pumping stations affected. Diesel generator costs elevated.

SAA negative equity of R5.6bn post-business rescue. Ongoing net losses threaten long-term viability. Additional government capital injection may be required if SEP process does not conclude successfully.
Fleet age and reliability risk: Average SAA fleet age 14.8 years. Multiple aircraft requiring heavy maintenance checks. Fleet availability impacting schedule reliability and customer experience.

Fuel price volatility: Jet fuel constitutes 32% of SAA operating costs. A 10% increase in jet fuel prices increases annual costs by R380m with limited hedging in place. Brent crude above $85/bbl is critical threshold.

SAA SACAA AOC compliance risk: Failure to maintain IOSA certification or meet SACAA safety directives could result in AOC suspension, grounding the airline. Current compliance adequate but resource-constrained.

Competition risk from low-cost carriers: Kulula, FlySafair and Lift have captured 52% of domestic market share since SAA's absence. Recovering market share requires substantial marketing and pricing investment.

Denel Pension Fund deficit of R2.5bn: Defined benefit obligations exceed fund assets. Company contributions deferred due to cash constraints 2019-2022 exacerbating the deficit. Actuarial recovery plan spans 15 years.

Critical skills exodus: Salary arrears 2020-2022 caused loss of 580 engineers and technical specialists. Average experience lost estimated at 18 years per departed employee. Capabilities in certain weapons systems now below critical mass.

Contract delivery risk: Rooivalk export contract 4-month delay creates R180m penalty exposure. Production capacity constraints from skills gaps and aging manufacturing equipment threaten future contract competitiveness.

Export market credibility: Multiple delivery delays and the salary crisis have damaged Denel's reputation as a reliable defence supplier. Rebuilding international market credibility requires sustained on-time delivery performance.

NCACC export compliance: Increasing complexity of international sanctions regimes requires rigorous end-user certificate verification. Risk of inadvertent export control violation with consequent criminal penalties.

Transmission entity capital requirements: NTCSA will require R180-220bn in capital investment over 10 years for grid expansion to accommodate 14,000MW of new renewable generation. Funding sources not fully committed.

Irregular expenditure legacy: R6.2bn in irregular expenditure identified in FY2021-FY2022 AGSA audits related to State Capture-era procurement. Consequence management processes slow. Legal recovery actions limited.

Rand/Dollar exchange rate risk: Aircraft leases, maintenance and fuel denominated in USD. A R1 depreciation against USD increases annual costs by approximately R120m. Limited natural hedging available.

Tariff cost-reflectivity gap: Even after NERSA 18.65% increase, tariff remains below full cost-reflective level by an estimated 8-12%. Cumulative deficit compounds annually reducing path to profitability.

Manufacturing facility condition risk: Several Denel facilities (Swartklip, Lyttleton) require major refurbishment. Deferred maintenance during financial crisis has elevated operational failure risk and safety incidents.

Customer Information System (CIS) has not received security patches since March 2023. Vulnerability scan identified 3 critical CVEs that could allow unauthorized access to billing data and payment systems for 7.2 million customers.

Supplier payment backlog of KES 8.5bn as at September 2024, including KES 3.2bn overdue to KenGen for power purchase obligations. Risk of service disruption if KenGen suspends supply under PPA default clause.

KPLC SAIDI performance of 38 hours per customer per year in FY2024 exceeds the EPRA minimum standard of 35 hours. If not improved to 35 hours by June 2025, EPRA may impose revenue clawback of up to KES 1.8bn per the regulatory framework.

High dependence on geothermal generation from Olkaria (contributing 42% of total supply) creates concentration risk. Any major outage at Olkaria would require expensive emergency thermal imports at up to 3x normal power purchase cost.

Foreign exchange exposure on USD-denominated loans totalling KES 38bn. KES/USD exchange rate depreciation of 15% in FY2024 added KES 5.7bn to loan balances. No formal forex hedging programme in place.
High voltage work-related accidents: 3 Lost Time Injuries (LTIs) recorded in H1 FY2024 including one fatality at Machakos substation during transformer replacement. OSHA investigation ongoing.

Nairobi CBD power blackout on 3 October 2024 affecting 420,000 customers for 7.75 hours received extensive negative media coverage and Parliamentary summons. Customer satisfaction index declined 8 points to 62%.

6 pending legal cases from contractors disputing contract terminations totalling KES 2.8bn in claimed damages. Risk assessment indicates KES 900m probable adverse outcome based on legal counsel opinion.

Technical and commercial losses (aggregate) at 22% of units distributed in FY2024, above the EPRA regulatory allowance of 18%. Excess losses of 4% represent unrecovered revenue of approximately KES 2.3bn annually.

Fuel hedging programme coverage expired 31 December 2024 with no renewal in place. At current Jet A-1 prices of KES 188/litre and unhedged consumption of 420 million litres, adverse price movement of 10% would represent a KES 1.8bn unfavourable variance versus budget.

Kenya Airways has negative equity of KES 36bn and debt/EBITDA ratio of 18x. Two lenders (Equity Bank and Standard Bank) have covenant breach provisions that could trigger acceleration of KES 12bn in debt if EBITDA does not reach KES 2bn by December 2024.

Average fleet age of 12.8 years (as at 2024) increasing maintenance costs. Three aircraft (2 B737-800 and 1 B777-300ER) are grounded awaiting heavy maintenance at a total cost of KES 2.8bn. Grounding reduces revenue-generating capacity by 9%.

Pilot fatigue incidents: 4 crew discretion (CDA) events reported in Q1-Q3 2024 where pilots declined to operate on fatigue grounds. While compliant with regulations, the frequency indicates crew scheduling deficiencies. KCAA has requested explanation.

CORSIA Phase 1 compliance — KQ's carbon emissions monitoring, reporting and verification (MRV) system was deployed only in January 2024. 2023 baseline emissions data may not be fully captured, creating a risk of non-compliance penalties from ICAO.

Revenue leakage in code-share settlement reconciliation identified by Ernst & Young audit. Estimated unreconciled revenue of KES 320m from Air France-KLM, Delta, and Ethiopian Airlines code-share prorate settlements since 2022.

Pilot shortage risk — KQ has 18 unfilled B787 type-rated pilot vacancies due to global pilot shortage and competition from Gulf carriers offering 40% higher salaries. Risk of route suspensions if unfilled by Q1 2025.

Kenya Airways ranked 98th out of 100 in Skytrax 2024 African airline ranking. Social media sentiment analysis shows 62% negative sentiment primarily related to flight delays (average departure delay of 45 minutes) and baggage handling complaints.

Kilindini Channel silting — survey conducted in September 2024 shows channel depth reduced to 13.2m in 3 sections, below the minimum 14m required for fully laden Panamax vessels. Emergency dredging required before end of year at estimated cost of KES 850m.

Bunker oil spill of 12 tonnes in Kilindini Harbour on 15 July 2024 from MV Atlantic Star (Greek-registered). KPA Port Health Authority and NEMA investigating. Potential MARPOL penalties and clean-up costs estimated at KES 45m. Civil liability exposure from fishermen claims.

KRA has raised a tax assessment of KES 420m relating to VAT on port handling charges for FY2021-2023, claiming exemption should not apply. KPA has filed an objection with the Tax Appeals Tribunal. Risk of adverse ruling requiring payment plus 2% monthly interest.

3 of 12 ship-to-shore gantry cranes are beyond their 25-year design life and require replacement by 2026. Failure of any crane during peak operations would reduce berth productivity by 25% and risk vessel diversions to Dar es Salaam.

Workplace accident rate of 4.2 per 1,000 employees in FY2024 (industry benchmark: 2.5). Three serious injuries in stevedoring operations involving crane operations. OSHA audit scheduled for December 2024.

Drought conditions reducing hydro generation capacity from Tana River cascade by an estimated 35% in FY2025. Emergency thermal imports required at 3x normal cost, with estimated additional power purchase cost of KES 4.2bn if dry conditions persist to June 2025.

Ethiopian Airlines has increased Nairobi-London and Nairobi-Amsterdam frequencies to twice daily, directly competing with KQ's core long-haul routes. KQ market share on Europe routes declined from 38% to 31% in 12 months.

Proposed East African Community Tripartite Port Policy changes could require KPA to adopt uniform pilotage fee schedules with Dar es Salaam Port, potentially reducing KPA pilotage revenue by KES 280m annually.
